mnemonics.title: PTC Error Detection Methods
mnemonics.content: PTC = Parity, Checksum, CRC - Remember: 'Please Throw Cookies' to detect transmission errors

mnemonics.title: Even Parity Rule
mnemonics.content: Even Parity: Total 1s including parity bit should be EVEN. 'All Friends Enjoy' (All = 0, Friends = 1, Enjoy = Even)

mnemonics.title: Odd Parity Rule
mnemonics.content: Odd Parity: Total 1s including parity bit should be ODD. 'On Odd Nights Parity Fails' (Parity bit makes total odd)

mnemonics.title: CRC Polynomial Division
mnemonics.content: CRC = Cyclic Redundancy Check - Remember: 'Cats Run Continuously' during polynomial division to detect errors

mnemonics.title: Hamming Code Distance
mnemonics.content: Hamming Distance formula: d_min = minimum number of bit differences between any two valid codewords. Detect d-1 errors, correct ⌊(d-1)/2⌋ errors

mnemonics.title: ARQ Protocol Types
mnemonics.content: ARQ = Automatic Repeat reQuest - Types: 'Stop Go Selective' (Stop-and-wait, Go-Back-N, Selective Repeat)

mnemonics.title: Sliding Window Flow
mnemonics.content: Sliding Window: 'Send Multiple Frames Before Waiting' - Window slides as acknowledgments arrive

mnemonics.title: Checksum Calculation
mnemonics.content: Checksum: Sum all 16-bit words using 1's complement, then take complement of result. 'Add All, Then Flip All Bits'

acronyms.usage: Most powerful error detection method; uses polynomial division; detects burst errors up to length of generator polynomial
Error control protocol that automatically requests retransmission of corrupted frames
Error detection field in data link layer frames; typically contains CRC remainder
Single parity bit added to data unit; simplest error detection method
Block check character; checks parity across multiple data units
Frame sent by receiver to confirm error-free data reception
Frame sent by receiver to indicate error detection and request retransmission
Ratio of erroneous bits to total bits transmitted

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a row of light bulbs (data bits). Add one extra bulb (parity bit) so total lit bulbs becomes even (for even parity). If any bulb fails, the count becomes odd and you know an error occurred.

Think of a spreadsheet table. Each row has a parity bit at the end, each column has a parity bit at the bottom. When error occurs, both row and column parity lights up red, pinpointing the exact error location like coordinates on a grid.
Imagine dividing polynomials like long division with XOR gates. The generator polynomial is your divisor. Remainder becomes FCS. If division doesn't divide evenly, error is detected.

Think of a magic square where positions 1,2,4,8,16... (powers of 2) are parity bits. Each parity bit checks specific groups of bits. The error position is the sum of all wrong parity bits - like finding a missing puzzle piece.
Imagine a horizontal conveyor belt (window) with slots. Frames slide in from left (sent) to right (acknowledged). As ACK arrives, window slides forward, opening new slots for new frames to send.

Think of a person sending postcards one at a time. They wait at the mailbox (stop) until they see the recipient's reply card (acknowledgment) before sending the next one. If reply doesn't come in time, send same card again.
Imagine sending 5 numbered boxes. If box #3 is damaged, you must go back and resend boxes 3, 4, and 5 - even if 4 and 5 were fine. You restart from the error point.
Same as Go-Back-N but smarter: if box #3 is damaged, you only resend box #3. Boxes 4 and 5 are kept in a 'pending' pile. Receiver accepts only the specific missing/repeated frame.

analogies.analogy: Like a pair of socks check before packing. If you have an even number of socks and one goes missing, you'll notice immediately (odd count = error detected).
Like adding up prices on a grocery receipt. If sum doesn't match expected total, you know something was mispriced or scanned wrong.
Like a sophisticated fingerprint for data. Just as each person has unique fingerprints, each data stream has a unique CRC signature that changes completely with any modification.

Like having multiple cross-referencing friends verify a story. Each friend (parity bit) checks different parts of the story. By knowing which friends' versions don't match, you can pinpoint and fix exactly what was misstated.
Like sending letters by registered mail. You send one letter, wait for delivery confirmation before sending the next. Simple but slow.
Like a restaurant table reservation system. You can have multiple tables reserved (window size) at once. As customers finish (ACK arrives), new reservations fill those spots.
Like replaying a playlist from a corrupted song. If song #3 skips, you restart the playlist from song #3. Inefficient but ensures correct order.
Like downloading a file with corrupted chunks. Download manager only re-downloads missing/corrupted chunks, not the entire file. Efficient and smart.
